refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly incorporates the measurement definition (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period concept, and acknowledgment that availability encompasses both planned and unplanned downtime. High safety criticality demands that safety-driven shutdowns are counted as legitimate availability losses, not masked.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold) with timestamped records; post-evaluation period analysis of uptime vs. total time; comparison against customer-defined baseline and acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all fault conditions that degrade or prevent mission capability within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall execute automated recovery procedures for transient faults to restore availability without operator intervention.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide predictive maintenance alerts to enable proactive component replacement before failure.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate failed subsystems to preserve partial mission capability when feasible.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions even during degraded availability states.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant hardware and software subsystems with automatic failover logic and continuous health monitoring across all critical paths.
DP-B: Deploy a modular architecture with fault isolation, graceful degradation, and hot-swap capability for high-wear components; couple with predictive maintenance scheduling.
DP-C: Establish a real-time telemetry and diagnostics pipeline that feeds a centralized availability analytics engine; use machine learning to predict failures and trigger preventive maintenance.
DP-D: Combine redundancy (DP-A), modularity with graceful degradation (DP-B), and predictive diagnostics (DP-C) into an integrated availability management system.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Integrated Availability Management System combining hardware/software redundancy with automatic failover, modular fault isolation and graceful degradation, and real-time predictive diagnostics to achieve ≥0.97 availability through prevention, detection, and rapid recovery.

potential_sources: Redundancy logic (DP-A) must coordinate with fault isolation (DP-B) to avoid conflicting recovery actions.
Predictive diagnostics (DP-C) must integrate with failover triggers (DP-A) to avoid false positives causing unnecessary switchovers.
Graceful degradation (DP-B) must preserve safety-critical functions even when non-critical subsystems are offline.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a coupled design that requires careful interface definition between redundancy, modularity, and diagnostics subsystems. Coupling is acceptable because the three mechanisms are complementary and necessary to meet the 0.97 target; decoupling them would reduce effectiveness. Mitigation: explicit interface contracts and integration testing.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant hardware and software can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance data (historical failure patterns, sensor baselines) is available or can be collected during early operation.
Risk: Redundancy adds weight, power consumption, and complexity; may conflict with other system requirements (e.g., fuel efficiency, operator workload).
Risk: Predictive diagnostics accuracy depends on data quality and model training; poor predictions could trigger unnecessary maintenance or miss real failures.
Risk: Failover logic may introduce latency or transient mission interruptions; safety-critical functions must remain unaffected.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) which subsystems are redundant vs. gracefully degraded vs. monitored-only; (2) failover latency budget and safety constraints; (3) predictive model confidence thresholds and fallback rules; (4) maintenance scheduling logic to minimize planned downtime.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The top-level system integration module that owns the end-to-end availability management system, including coordination of redundancy, fault isolation, graceful degradation, and predictive diagnostics across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and performance data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; feeds predictive diagnostics engine and availability analytics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional mission commands and status; NGHE Solution modulates tasking based on availability state (e.g., reduces task complexity during degraded mode).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety-critical alerts and shutdown commands; NGHE Solution ensures safety functions remain active even during availability recovery.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for telemetry, tasking, and safety alerts; NGHE Solution monitors network health as part of availability assessment.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration point responsible for coordinating availability across all subsystems. Availability KPI is a system-level concern that requires end-to-end visibility and control. Minimal diffusion: availability logic is centralized in this module with clear interfaces to subsystems.

Moderate containment: Vehicle owns hardware redundancy and fault isolation for propulsion, steering, and mechanical subsystems. However, availability also depends on Teleoperation and Safety Services; assigning DP-D entirely to Vehicle would fragment the availability strategy.

Moderate-low containment: Teleoperation owns remote control and communication redundancy, but availability is not solely a teleoperation concern. Assigning DP-D here would miss Vehicle and Safety Services contributions.

Moderate containment: Safety Services owns safety-critical monitoring and shutdown logic, which is essential for maintaining availability during degraded states. However, Safety Services does not own the full availability management system (e.g., predictive diagnostics, failover logic for non-safety subsystems).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the integrated availability management system because: (1) it has system-level visibility across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) availability is a system-level KPI that cannot be achieved by any single subsystem in isolation; (3) it owns the predictive diagnostics engine and failover orchestration logic; (4) it interfaces with all known interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) required to implement DP-D.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if availability logic is too centralized; subsystems may lack autonomy to respond to local faults.
Risk: Telemetry volume and latency could exceed network capacity if all health data flows through NGHE Solution; distributed health monitoring may be needed.
Risk: Safety Services must retain veto power over availability decisions (e.g., refuse to operate in unsafe degraded modes); NGHE Solution must respect safety constraints.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define a clear separation of concerns: NGHE Solution owns system-level availability orchestration and predictive diagnostics; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own local fault detection and graceful degradation within their domains. Establish explicit contracts (e.g., health status schemas, failover triggers, safety constraints) to enable distributed decision-making while maintaining system-level availability targets.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Integrated Availability Management System) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability) through three complementary mechanisms: redundancy prevents single-point failures, graceful degradation preserves partial capability, and predictive diagnostics enable proactive maintenance. The DP is measurable (availability ratio), achievable (proven technologies), and aligned with high safety criticality (safety functions remain active during recovery).

diffusion_risks: Risk: If availability logic is split across NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without clear contracts, redundancy and failover decisions may conflict or duplicate effort.
Risk: Predictive diagnostics engine in NGHE Solution may not have sufficient visibility into Vehicle subsystem internals; requires rich telemetry interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because it has system-level scope and interfaces with all required external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will implement local fault detection and graceful degradation; NGHE Solution orchestrates system-level recovery. This distribution is acceptable because it balances centralized availability strategy with distributed autonomy.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified through continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation analysis. Predictive diagnostics accuracy can be validated through historical data replay and field trials. Failover logic can be tested through fault injection and simulation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution with sub-allocations to Vehicle (hardware redundancy, fault isolation), Teleoperation (communication redundancy), and Safety Services (safety-critical monitoring). Each sub-allocation has clear success criteria (e.g., failover latency <100ms, predictive accuracy >90%).

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit interface contracts between NGHE Solution and subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to define health status schemas, failover triggers, and safety constraints.
Define availability targets for each subsystem (e.g., Vehicle availability ≥0.98, Teleoperation availability ≥0.99) to ensure system-level target is achievable.
Specify failover latency budgets and safety-critical function preservation requirements to ensure high safety criticality is maintained during recovery.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-functional requirements (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.5) that address specific availability mechanisms (fault detection, automated recovery, predictive maintenance, fault isolation, safety preservation). DP-D is a coupled design that integrates three complementary mechanisms; further refinement is needed to allocate each mechanism to specific subsystems and define integration contracts. NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner, but subsystem-level DPs and IMs must be defined to realize the system-level DP.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all fault conditions that degrade or prevent mission capability within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall execute automated recovery procedures for transient faults to restore availability without operator intervention.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide predictive maintenance alerts to enable proactive component replacement before failure.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate failed subsystems to preserve partial mission capability when feasible.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions even during degraded availability states.

assumptions: Redundant hardware and software can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Predictive maintenance data (historical failure patterns, sensor baselines) is available or can be collected during early operation.
Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can provide rich telemetry (health status, performance metrics) to support predictive diagnostics.
Failover logic can be implemented with latency <100ms to avoid mission interruption.
Safety Services retains veto power over availability decisions to ensure safety is never compromised for availability.
open_risks: Redundancy adds weight, power consumption, and complexity; may conflict with other system requirements (e.g., fuel efficiency, operator workload).
Predictive diagnostics accuracy depends on data quality and model training; poor predictions could trigger unnecessary maintenance or miss real failures.
Failover logic may introduce latency or transient mission interruptions; safety-critical functions must remain unaffected.
Telemetry volume and latency could exceed network capacity if all health data flows through NGHE Solution; distributed health monitoring may be needed.
Availability logic split across multiple modules without clear contracts could lead to conflicting or duplicate recovery actions.
evidence_needed: Historical failure data for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to validate predictive diagnostics models.
Failover latency measurements and safety impact analysis to confirm <100ms latency and safety preservation.
Cost and weight impact analysis of redundancy to ensure feasibility within system constraints.
Network capacity analysis to confirm telemetry bandwidth is sufficient for real-time diagnostics.
Integration test results demonstrating coordinated failover across subsystems without conflicts or safety violations.
